messages.content:
Three ants walk on a thin rod 100 cm long.
When an ant reaches either end of the rod it falls off. When two ants meet,
they BOUNCE: both instantly reverse direction, and each keeps its own speed.

Starting positions and velocities (positive = toward the 100 cm end):

- Ant A: at 10 cm, moving +3 cm/s
- Ant B: at 40 cm, moving -1 cm/s
- Ant C: at 80 cm, moving -2 cm/s

After how many seconds does the LAST ant fall off the rod? Give the exact
number of seconds.


When you are finished, output your final answer on its own line, as the last line of your response, in the exact format:
ANSWER: <your answer>

text:
The classic trick: bouncing ants are equivalent to ants passing through each other (since after collision the *set* of positions/velocities is identical to swapping which ant carries which label). So track "ghost" particles that pass through unimpeded.

- Ghost A: x = 10+3t → hits 100 at t=30
- Ghost B: x = 40−t → hits 0 at t=40
- Ghost C: x = 80−2t → hits 0 at t=40

The set of exit times for the three ants (physical, just relabeled) is {30, 40, 40}. So the last ant falls off at t = 40 seconds.

ANSWER: 40 seconds
